message.content: In the /worksp/hiclaw project, find the chat interface component(s) where messages are displayed. I'm looking for the component that renders chat messages - both user messages and system/manager messages. Find the file paths and the relevant JSX/HTML where messages are rendered, including any CSS classes or styled-components used for message styling. Search breadth: medium.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	#!/bin/bash
2	# start-element-web.sh - Generate Element Web config and start Nginx
3	
4	MATRIX_DOMAIN="${HICLAW_MATRIX_DOMAIN:-matrix-local.hiclaw.io:8080}"
5	# Browser-facing homeserver URL.
6	# If the configured value is blank, loopback, or plain HTTP, fall back to the
7	# current request origin so Element talks to the same public HTTPS host that
8	# served the app.
9	ELEMENT_HOMESERVER_URL="${HICLAW_ELEMENT_HOMESERVER_URL:-}"
10	case "${ELEMENT_HOMESERVER_URL}" in
11	    ""|http://*|https://127.0.0.1*|https://localhost*|http://127.0.0.1*|http://localhost*)
12	        ELEMENT_HOMESERVER_URL="__PUBLIC_BASE_URL__"
13	        ;;
14	esac
15	# Brand name for Element Web (defaults to "Element" if not set)
16	ELEMENT_BRAND="${HICLAW_ELEMENT_BRAND:-Element}"
17	
18	# Generate Element Web config.json pointing to local Matrix Homeserver
19	cat > /opt/element-web/config.json << EOF
20	{
21	    "default_server_config": {
22	        "m.homeserver": {
23	            "base_url": "${ELEMENT_HOMESERVER_URL}"
24	        }
25	    },
26	    "brand": "${ELEMENT_BRAND}",
27	    "disable_guests": true,
28	    "disable_custom_urls": false,
29	    "force_verification": false,
30	    "setting_defaults": {
31	        "e2ee.manuallyVerifyAllSessions": false,
32	        "UIFeature.advancedEncryption": false,
33	        "UIFeature.accessTokens": false
34	    },
35	    "e2ee": {
36	        "secure_backup_setup_at_login": false,
37	        "secure_backup_required": false
38	    }
39	}
40	EOF
41	
42	# Configure nginx worker processes (default is auto, which uses CPU core count)
43	sed -i 's/worker_processes.*auto;/worker_processes 2;/' /etc/nginx/nginx.conf 2>/dev/null || \
44	sed -i 's/^worker_processes [0-9]*;/worker_processes 2;/' /etc/nginx/nginx.conf 2>/dev/null || \
45	grep -q '^worker_processes' /etc/nginx/nginx.conf || \
46	sed -i '1i worker_processes 2;' /etc/nginx/nginx.conf
47	
48	# Create browser bypass script as external JS file (allowed by CSP script-src 'self')
49	# This avoids adding 'unsafe-inline' to CSP, preserving XSS protection
50	echo 'window.localStorage.setItem("mx_accepts_unsupported_browser","true");' > /opt/element-web/browser-bypass.js

1	# HiClaw Host Ops
2	
3	Host-side infrastructure layer for a running HiClaw deployment. **Not** the HiClaw application source — this repo owns the surrounding keeper scripts, Docker image, and routing config that keep it alive.
4	
5	**Read [AGENTS.md](AGENTS.md) for the full operating guide.**
6	
7	## What This Is
8	
9	HiClaw is an AI agent orchestration platform (by Alibaba/Higress) running on a single Linux server (`178.156.180.212`). Agents receive tasks via Matrix/Element chat, execute them using DeepSeek via OpenRouter, and automate a live Chrome browser via CDP. The OpenClaw gateway (inside `hiclaw-manager`) handles the agent runtime. Google OAuth gates all web-facing services.
10	
11	## Key URLs
12	
13	| Service | URL |
14	|---|---|
15	| Element chat UI | https://claw.designflow.app |
16	| Control panel (OpenClaw) | https://control.claw.designflow.app |
17	| Gateway API | https://gateway.claw.designflow.app |
18	| noVNC desktop | https://vnc.designflow.app |
19	
20	## Container Quick Reference
21	
22	| Container | Purpose | Status |
23	|---|---|---|
24	| `hiclaw-controller` | Orchestration, MinIO, Higress AI gateway, Element Web, Matrix (Tuwunel) | `docker logs hiclaw-controller --since 5m` |
25	| `hiclaw-manager` | Agent runtime, OpenClaw gateway, Matrix integration | `docker logs hiclaw-manager --since 5m` |
26	| `novnc-desktop` | Chrome via noVNC for CDP browser automation | `docker ps --filter name=novnc-desktop` |
27	| `oauth2-proxy` | Google OAuth gate for all web services | `docker ps --filter name=oauth2-proxy` |
28	
29	`hiclaw-controller` and `hiclaw-manager` are **not in Coolify** — managed by keeper scripts as cron jobs.
30	
31	## Emergency Recovery
32	
33	```bash
34	# openclaw.json corrupted? Restore from backup:
35	cp /worksp/hiclaw/workspace/openclaw.json.bak /worksp/hiclaw/workspace/openclaw.json
36	
37	# Check all containers are up:
38	docker ps --format "{{.Names}}\t{{.Status}}"
39	
40	# Check keeper crons are registered:
41	sudo crontab -u ai -l
42	```
43	
44	## Docs
45	
46	- [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md) — system design and data flow
47	- [docs/configuration.md](docs/configuration.md) — all env vars and config files
48	- [docs/development.md](docs/development.md) — debugging and safe edit workflows
49	- [docs/deployment.md](docs/deployment.md) — deploy, upgrade, and recovery procedures

content.content:
1	# Architecture
2	
3	## 1. System Overview
4	
5	HiClaw is a personal AI agent orchestration platform running on a single dedicated Linux server (`178.156.180.212`). The owner (Albert) interacts with an AI agent exclusively through a Matrix/Element chat interface. The agent receives natural-language tasks, executes them using a cloud LLM (DeepSeek via OpenRouter), and automates a live Chrome browser for web tasks using Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP).
6	
7	This repository does not contain the HiClaw application itself. It owns:
8	- One Docker image (`novnc-desktop`) that provides a browser-in-a-box via noVNC
9	- Host-side keeper scripts that maintain container state across restarts
10	- OAuth2 proxy configuration that gates all web-facing services with Google login
11	- Traefik dynamic routing configuration
12	
13	The HiClaw controller and manager images are maintained by Alibaba/Higress and are not built from this repository.
14	
15	---
16	
17	## 2. Component Diagram
18	
19	```
20	Browser (Albert's laptop)
21	        |
22	        v  HTTPS (443)
23	  coolify-proxy (Traefik v3.6)
24	        |
25	        |-- /oauth2/*  ---> oauth2-proxy:4180 (Google OAuth callbacks, no auth)
26	        |
27	        +-- claw.designflow.app  ---[google-auth]---> hiclaw-controller:8088
28	        |   (Element Web, excl /_matrix)                    |
29	        |                                                   | nginx sub_filter
30	        |                                                   | injects JS bundles
31	        |                                                   v
32	        |                                          Element Web SPA
33	        |                                          auto-login.js --> POST /hiclaw-api/session
34	        |                                                              |
35	        |                                                              v
36	        |                                                   hiclaw-chat-api.py :8091
37	        |                                                   (Matrix password login)
38	        |
39	        +-- claw.designflow.app/_matrix  ---> hiclaw-controller:8080
40	        |   (Matrix homeserver API, no auth)        |
41	        |                                           v
42	        |                                      Tuwunel :6167 (Matrix homeserver)
43	        |
44	        +-- control.claw.designflow.app  --[google-auth]--> hiclaw-controller:18888
45	        |   (OpenClaw manager console)                          |
46	        |                                                       | nginx proxy (Docker DNS resolver)
47	        |                                                       v
48	        |                                              hiclaw-manager:18799
49	        |                                              (OpenClaw gateway)
50	        |
51	        +-- gateway.claw.designflow.app  --[google-auth]--> hiclaw-manager:18799
52	        |   (OpenClaw direct gateway access)
53	        |
54	        +-- vnc.designflow.app  ---> novnc-desktop:3000
55	            (noVNC browser view, no auth)
56	
57	Internal (hiclaw-net) service mesh:

170	## 5. Data Flows
171	
172	### 5.1 Matrix message becomes an AI response
173	
174	```
175	Albert types in Element Web (claw.designflow.app)
176	  → POST to Tuwunel /_matrix/client/v3/rooms/.../send (via claw.designflow.app/_matrix route)
177	  → Tuwunel stores message in RocksDB, pushes sync event
178	  → hiclaw-manager Matrix channel plugin receives sync event via long-poll
179	  → OpenClaw gateway routes to active agent session (agent:main:main)
180	  → Agent calls LLM: POST http://aigw-local.hiclaw.io:8080/v1/chat/completions
181	       (aigw-local.hiclaw.io resolves to hiclaw-controller inside hiclaw-net)
182	  → Higress (Envoy) applies AI plugin (auth, retry, model routing)
183	  → Higress forwards to OpenRouter https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
184	  → OpenRouter calls DeepSeek/deepseek-v4-pro
185	  → Response streams back through Higress → OpenClaw → Matrix channel plugin
186	  → POST to Tuwunel to send reply message
187	  → Element Web receives reply via sync
188	```
189	
190	For browser automation tasks, the agent additionally:
191	```
192	  Agent calls Playwright MCP tool
193	  → OpenClaw MCP client connects to http://10.0.5.4:9223 (CDP endpoint)
194	  → cdp_proxy.py on novnc-desktop bridges to Chrome :9222
195	  → Chrome executes the automation
196	```
197	
198	### 5.2 openclaw.json config change flow
199	
200	`openclaw.json` is a live config file with three concurrent writers:
201	
202	```
203	Writer 1: hiclaw-controller ManagerReconciler
204	  - Runs every ~47 seconds
205	  - Writes its template values (model list, commands field, channels config)
206	  - Can write invalid schema (channels.matrix.groups.*.allow instead of .enabled)
207	  - Can write null for fields the manager added (YOLO settings, tool config)
208	
209	Writer 2: OpenClaw gateway (hiclaw-manager)
210	  - Writes on config-change events (plugin state, runtime settings)
211	  - Writes config-health.json to track "last known good" hash
212	
213	Writer 3: manager-config-keeper.sh (host cron, every ~60s)
214	  - Normalizes schema issues from Writer 1
215	  - Enforces invariants (bootstrapMaxChars, contextWindow, clawtalk/restaurant-cli entries)
216	  - Defeats observe-recovery by updating config-health.json with new hash
217	  - Atomic write to prevent partial-read by the gateway file watcher
218	
219	Flow on a typical reconciler cycle:
220	  1. Reconciler writes openclaw.json with allow keys, wildcard groups, possibly bad model data, and commands drift
221	  2. Gateway file watcher fires and evaluates the config
222	  3. keeper runs: normalizes allow→enabled, removes wildcard groups, clears commands.restart if present, updates config-health.json
223	  4. Gateway file watcher fires again → hot reload only (no restart, no SIGUSR1)
224	```
225	
226	**Why `commands.restart` must not persist:**
227	The gateway diffs live config changes against the startup baseline recorded in `config-health.json`. In the current stable state, `start-manager-agent.sh` deletes `commands.restart` before launch and `manager-config-keeper.sh` clears it if it reappears. Persisting a restart command can turn routine reconciler/config writes into recurring SIGUSR1 restarts.
228	
229	### 5.3 Manager startup sequence
230	
231	On every container start, `start-manager-agent.sh` runs as the container entrypoint:
232	
233	1. **Wait for dependencies** — polls Higress (:8080, :8001), Tuwunel (:6167), MinIO (:9000)
234	2. **Load secrets** — reads `/data/hiclaw-secrets.env`; auto-generates and persists `MANAGER_GATEWAY_KEY` and `MANAGER_PASSWORD` if absent
235	3. **MinIO pull (k8s mode)** — `mc mirror hiclaw/hiclaw-storage/manager/ /root/manager-workspace/` with exclusions to prevent recursion
236	4. **Workspace versioning** — checks `.builtin-version`; runs `upgrade-builtins.sh` on version change
237	5. **Matrix account setup** — registers admin + manager accounts (idempotent), obtains access token
238	6. **Higress initialization** — registers Matrix and Element Web service sources, creates LLM provider and AI route
239	7. **DM room creation** — finds or creates the admin DM room; on first boot schedules a welcome message
240	8. **openclaw.json update** — jq merge of known models, model metadata sync from OpenRouter, Matrix token update, schema migrations, `del(.pricing)` cleanup, push back to MinIO
241	9. **OpenRouter model sync** — writes API response to `/tmp/openrouter-models.json` (file, not variable, to avoid arg-length limits), updates contextWindow/maxTokens for matching models
242	10. **MinIO push** — immediately pushes updated `openclaw.json` to MinIO to prevent background sync from overwriting fresh values
243	11. **ClawTalk bootstrap** — patches plugin manifest, writes the host-backed bundled shim at `/root/manager-workspace/.openclaw/bundled-extensions/clawtalk/`, symlinks `/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/extensions/clawtalk/` to it, clears `installs.json`
244	12. **WhatsApp bootstrap** — installs `@openclaw/whatsapp` if absent, ensures config entries
245	13. **Fake systemd-run install** — writes `/usr/local/bin/systemd-run` wrapper
246	14. **openclaw validation** — checks npm-installed openclaw for `json5/package.json` AND `openai/index.mjs`; removes broken install and falls back to `/opt/openclaw/` if either is missing
247	15. **restaurant-cli bootstrap** — installs `restaurant-cli@0.1.18` if absent, links the active OpenClaw peer dependency, writes the host-backed bundled shim at `/root/manager-workspace/.openclaw/bundled-extensions/restaurant-cli/`, exposes `/usr/local/bin/restaurant`, copies the restaurant skill into the manager workspace, installs/starts `at` when available, and clears `installs.json`
248	16. **Hash recording** — writes openclaw package.json hash to `.openclaw-startup-pkg-hash`
249	17. **`exec openclaw gateway run`** — replaces the shell process
250	
251	### 5.4 Google SSO auto-login flow
252	
253	After passing Google OAuth via oauth2-proxy, the user's browser loads Element Web. `auto-login.js` (injected by nginx `sub_filter`) intercepts the page load:
254	
255	1. Checks `localStorage` for `mx_access_token` + `mx_user_id`. If present, exits — already logged in.
256	2. POSTs to `/hiclaw-api/session` → `hiclaw-chat-api.py` → Matrix password login with `device_id=hiclaw_web_auto` (fixed, not random)
257	3. Writes 7 `mx_*` keys into `localStorage` directly
258	4. Calls `window.location.replace('/')` — triggers Element's restore-session path, which skips cross-signing verification
259	
260	Using `loginToken` URL parameter was tried and abandoned: it creates a new Matrix device each time, which triggers Element's "verify this device" cross-signing screen with no usable skip path. The fixed `device_id` approach reuses the same device record, making every login appear as a session restore rather than a new login.

313	## 7. Update Mechanism
314	
315	### Normal flow (via Control UI "Update now")
316	
317	```
318	1. Admin clicks "Update now" in control.claw.designflow.app
319	2. OpenClaw calls update.run in-process
320	3. Because OPENCLAW_SYSTEMD_UNIT=openclaw-gateway is set, openclaw follows
321	   managed-service-handoff path → calls /usr/local/bin/systemd-run
322	4. Fake systemd-run writes /root/manager-workspace/.openclaw-update-requested
323	   (host-visible as /worksp/hiclaw/workspace/.openclaw-update-requested)
324	   and exits 0
325	5. Gateway thinks handoff started; sends itself SIGUSR1 → in-process restart begins
326	6. manager-bootstrap-keeper.sh (host cron, ~60s interval) detects marker file:
327	   a. Removes marker
328	   b. Runs: docker exec hiclaw-manager openclaw update --yes --json
329	      (--memory-swap 3g is set via docker update before this step)
330	   c. Sleeps 30s (allows in-process SIGUSR1 write to complete — prevents truncation)
331	7. Keeper computes hash of new /usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/package.json
332	8. Hash differs from .openclaw-startup-pkg-hash → docker restart hiclaw-manager
333	9. start-manager-agent.sh validates new install (json5 + openai/index.mjs checks)
334	10. If valid: symlinks /usr/local/bin/openclaw → /usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/
335	    If invalid: removes broken install, symlinks to /opt/openclaw/ (base image)
336	11. New hash written to .openclaw-startup-pkg-hash
337	```
338	
339	**Why the 30s sleep is critical:** The `docker restart` immediately after `openclaw update` killed the container while openclaw was mid-write to `openclaw.json` during its SIGUSR1 in-process restart. This produced consistently truncated config files (8788 bytes vs 9283 bytes complete). The truncation corrupted config on every update cycle. The 30s sleep gives the write time to complete before the container is killed.
340	
341	### SIGUSR1 in-process restart vs docker restart
342	
343	| Event | Mechanism | openclaw.json written? | Container killed? |
344	|-------|-----------|----------------------|------------------|
345	| Config file change detected by file watcher | SIGUSR1 in-process | Yes (rewrites with current config) | No |
346	| `openclaw update --yes` | SIGUSR1 in-process | Yes (rewrites with new config) | No |
347	| Hash mismatch detected by keeper | `docker restart` | No (container is killed) | Yes |
348	| Manual `docker restart hiclaw-manager` | — | No | Yes |
349	
350	`OPENCLAW_NO_RESPAWN=1` is set in the environment, which changes openclaw's restart behavior: on SIGUSR1, it restarts in-process (re-requiring all modules) rather than calling `exec` to replace the process. This is required for the fake systemd-run handoff to work correctly, but it means config writes happen mid-restart and are vulnerable to interruption by `docker restart`.
351	
352	### Keeper orchestration
353	
354	Host-side keeper scripts maintain container/runtime state:
355	
356	| Script | Interval | Responsibility |
357	|--------|----------|---------------|
358	| `manager-bootstrap-keeper.sh` | ~60s | Sync start-manager-agent.sh into container; apply resource limits; consume update marker; detect version hash change |
359	| `manager-config-keeper.sh` | ~60s | Normalize openclaw.json; enforce invariants; defeat observe-recovery |
360	| `controller-bootstrap-keeper.sh` | ~60s | Sync start-element-web.sh and start-tuwunel.sh into controller; apply resource limits; restart if scripts changed |
361	| `novnc-resource-keeper.sh` | ~60s | Enforce noVNC Docker limits; restart before Chrome memory or PID growth can cause global OOM |
362	| `mcp-keeper.sh` | ~few min | Ensure mcp.servers.browser (Playwright CDP) is in openclaw.json |

366	## 8. OpenClaw Plugin Architecture
367	
368	OpenClaw loads plugins from two locations:
369	
370	1. **Bundled extensions:** `/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/extensions/<plugin>/`
371	2. **Load paths:** `plugins.load.paths` in `openclaw.json` — additional directories scanned for plugin manifests
372	
373	Plugins are declared in `plugins.entries` (with `enabled`, `apiKey`, `autoConnect` fields) and must appear in `plugins.allow` to be activated.
374	
375	### Matrix channel plugin
376	
377	Built into OpenClaw. Configured via `channels.matrix` in `openclaw.json`:
378	- Connects to Tuwunel at `http://hiclaw-controller:6167`
379	- Authenticates as `@manager:matrix-local.hiclaw.io:18080`
380	- Handles DMs from `allowFrom` list (`@admin`, `@manager`)
381	- `session.dmScope=main` collapses all DMs into `agent:main:main` session
382	
383	### clawtalk plugin
384	
385	- **npm package:** `/root/manager-workspace/.openclaw/npm/node_modules/clawtalk/`
386	- **Problem:** `build/index.js` is an ES module; OpenClaw uses CJS `require()`
387	- **Fix:** `start-manager-agent.sh` creates a CJS wrapper `index.cjs` that does `const m = require('./build/index.js'); module.exports = m.default || m;` and updates `package.json` to point at `index.cjs`
388	- **Host-backed bundled shim:** created at `/root/manager-workspace/.openclaw/bundled-extensions/clawtalk/`; `/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/extensions/clawtalk/` is a startup-created symlink to this workspace path
389	- **installs.json:** deleted on every startup so OpenClaw rescans and finds the shim; without deletion the cached registry predates the shim and reports "plugin not found"
390	- **Durability:** shim contents live in the host-mounted workspace; only the `/usr/lib/.../extensions/clawtalk` symlink is recreated inside the container overlay on startup
391	
392	### whatsapp plugin
393	
394	- **npm package:** `@openclaw/whatsapp` — installed at openclaw version if absent
395	- Configured in `plugins.allow`, `plugins.load.paths`, and `channels.whatsapp`
396	- `dmPolicy=pairing`, `groupPolicy=allowlist`
397	
398	### restaurant-cli plugin
399	
400	- **npm package:** `restaurant-cli@0.1.18` — installed into `/root/manager-workspace/.openclaw/npm/node_modules/restaurant-cli/` if absent
401	- **OpenClaw tools:** `restaurant_search`, `restaurant_availability`, `restaurant_book`, `restaurant_cancel`, `restaurant_list`, `restaurant_schedule_snipe`
402	- **Host-backed bundled shim:** created at `/root/manager-workspace/.openclaw/bundled-extensions/restaurant-cli/`; `/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/extensions/restaurant-cli/` is a startup-created symlink to this workspace path
403	- **Config:** enabled through `plugins.allow` and `plugins.entries.restaurant-cli`; the direct npm path is removed from `plugins.load.paths`
404	- **Skill:** copied from the package to `/root/manager-workspace/skills/restaurant/` on startup so agents can discover reservation workflows
405	- **Scheduler:** `restaurant_schedule_snipe` uses POSIX `at`; startup installs/starts `at` when the Ubuntu package manager is available
406	- **Peer dependency:** startup links the active OpenClaw install into the workspace npm tree so the package can resolve `openclaw/plugin-sdk/plugin-entry`
407	
408	### memory-core
409	
410	Built into OpenClaw. Activated when `memorySearch` is configured in `openclaw.json` with an embedding model (set from `HICLAW_EMBEDDING_MODEL`).
411	
412	### MCP browser plugin (Playwright)
413	
414	Not a traditional OpenClaw plugin — configured as `mcp.servers.browser` in `openclaw.json`:
415	```json
416	{
417	  "mcp": {
418	    "servers": {
419	      "browser": {
420	        "command": "npx",
421	        "args": ["@playwright/mcp", "--cdp-endpoint", "http://10.0.5.4:9223"]
422	      }
423	    }
424	  }
425	}
426	```
427	The OpenClaw gateway strips unknown top-level keys during MinIO sync. `mcp-keeper.sh` re-injects this config when it disappears. `fix-element-config.sh` installs an `mc` wrapper that injects it on every `mc cp` touching `openclaw.json`.
428	
429	---
430	
431	## 9. Key Constraints and Design Decisions
432	
433	### Workspace is a shared mutable file system
434	
435	The bind mount makes `/worksp/hiclaw/workspace/` visible to both containers and the host simultaneously. Three concurrent writers (`manager-config-keeper.sh`, the controller reconciler, and the OpenClaw gateway) write `openclaw.json` without coordination. The keeper's atomic write + `config-health.json` update is the only synchronization mechanism. This is a known constraint, not a bug to fix.
436	
437	### hiclaw-manager and hiclaw-controller are not in Coolify
438	
439	The containers require a shared volume mount (`/worksp/hiclaw/workspace`) that Coolify's compose model does not accommodate for this image version, plus startup-time environment injection and config patching baked into the keeper scripts. Migrating to Coolify would require rewriting the startup logic as compose-compatible entrypoints. This is deferred work.
440	
441	### openclaw.json reconciler conflicts are structural
442	
443	The hiclaw-controller ManagerReconciler writes its own template values to `openclaw.json` every ~47 seconds. This behavior is inside the upstream image and cannot be changed. The keeper scripts are the permanent mitigation: they run after the reconciler, normalize the schema, and update `config-health.json` to prevent observe-recovery from reverting the fixes.
444	
445	### MinIO recursion is a standing hazard
446	
447	The controller's reconciler pushes the workspace to MinIO. Because the manager's startup pull writes into the same workspace, if the exclusions are removed or circumvented, the workspace will contain a MinIO path that gets pushed back, creating recursive nesting. The `--exclude "hiclaw/*"` guard in `start-manager-agent.sh` is the only thing preventing this. Do not remove it.
448	
449	### cdp_proxy.py must be edited in-place
450	
451	`cdp_proxy.py` is bind-mounted into `novnc-desktop`. Docker bind mounts track the inode, not the path. Replacing the file (using Write tool, `cp`, or `mv`) creates a new inode; the container continues reading from the original inode and never sees the update. Always edit using the Edit tool (in-place modification) or `sed -i`.
452	
453	### commands.restart must not persist
454	
455	The gateway records its startup config as the baseline in `config-health.json` and triggers SIGUSR1 on diffs that require restart. The current startup script deletes `commands.restart` before launch, and the config keeper removes it if present. This keeps routine reconciler/config writes from being interpreted as restart signals.
456	
457	### YOLO settings removed from startup config
458	
459	Earlier versions of `start-manager-agent.sh` wrote `tools.exec`, `tools.elevated`, and `agents.defaults.elevatedDefault` to `openclaw.json` at startup. The v1.1.2 ManagerReconciler writes `null` for these fields approximately every 47 seconds. This produced a continuous diff → SIGUSR1 → restart loop making control.claw unavailable ~40% of the time. These settings were removed from the startup write; the reconciler baseline now matches the reconciler output.
460	
461	### Auto-login uses localStorage injection, not loginToken
462	
463	The `loginToken` URL parameter creates a new Matrix device on every use, triggering Element's cross-signing verification screen. There is no programmatic skip path. The fixed `device_id=hiclaw_web_auto` approach reuses the same device record, bypassing verification entirely after the first login. This is specific to single-admin deployments.
464	
465	---
466	
467	## 10. Scope Boundary
468	
469	**Owned by this repo:**
470	- `novnc-desktop/` — Docker image (Chrome, noVNC, CDP proxy)
471	- All `.sh` scripts at repo root — keeper and startup scripts
472	- `oauth2-proxy/` — auth proxy config
473	- `traefik/claw.yml` — routing rules
474	
475	**Not owned (upstream, read-only):**
476	- `hiclaw-controller` and `hiclaw-manager` images — Alibaba/Higress
477	- OpenClaw gateway inside hiclaw-manager
478	- Tuwunel internal state at `/data/tuwunel/`
479	- MinIO data model and reconciliation logic
480	
481	**Ephemeral (in-container, lost on restart, recreated by startup scripts):**
482	- clawtalk package manifest wrapper inside the npm package, recreated by startup if needed
483	- Fake `/usr/local/bin/systemd-run`
484	- `/usr/local/bin/openclaw` symlink
485	- ClawTalk bundled shim symlink at `/usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/extensions/clawtalk/` (points to host-backed workspace content)
486	- Element Web `config.json`, all injected JS files, nginx configs (regenerated by `start-element-web.sh`)
487

Based on my research, I can now provide you with a comprehensive answer about the chat interface components in this HiClaw project.

## Summary

The HiClaw project uses **Element Web** as its chat interface frontend, running on Nginx at `https://claw.designflow.app`. The standard Element Web chat messaging component is not a custom-built component in this codebase - Element Web is an upstream, pre-built Matrix client.

### Key Files and Components:

**1. Chat Interface Frontend Setup:**
- **Location:** `/worksp/hiclaw/start-element-web.sh`
- **Purpose:** Generates Element Web configuration and injects custom JavaScript into the Element Web SPA
- The script configures Element Web to point to the local Matrix homeserver (Tuwunel at `http://hiclaw-controller:6167`)

**2. Custom JavaScript Injections (chat UI enhancements):**

All custom scripts are generated and injected via nginx `sub_filter` directives in start-element-web.sh. These files are created in `/opt/element-web/`:

- **`auto-login.js`** (lines 56-77 in start-element-web.sh)
  - Handles automatic session restoration after Google OAuth
  - Injects Matrix session tokens directly into `localStorage` to skip verification screens
  - No JSX rendering; purely session management via localStorage API

- **`auth-ui-tweaks.js`** (lines 102-235)
  - Customizes Google OAuth button appearance with Material Design styling
  - Auto-skips device verification screens
  - CSS applied inline via `button.style.cssText` properties
  - Uses MutationObserver to detect DOM changes and inject styling dynamically

- **`control-panel-btn.js`** (lines 79-100)
  - Injects a fixed-position "Control Panel" button
  - CSS: Green background (#0dbd8b), positioned fixed at `left:70px;bottom:12px;width:232px;z-index:9000`
  - No JSX; vanilla DOM manipulation

- **`new-chat-btn.js`** (lines 237-333)
  - Injects "+ New Chat" button for creating new Matrix rooms
  - CSS: Blue background (#155eef), positioned fixed at `left:70px;bottom:56px`
  - Calls `/hiclaw-api/new-chat` endpoint to create rooms
  - Uses TreeWalker DOM API to find and click newly created rooms

**3. Backend API for Chat Operations:**
- **`hiclaw-chat-api.py`** (lines 335+, embedded in start-element-web.sh)
  - Python HTTP server running on `:8091` inside hiclaw-controller
  - Handles:
    - `/hiclaw-api/session` - POST for auto-login (password-based Matrix login)
    - `/hiclaw-api/new-chat` - POST to create new chat rooms with the manager agent
  - Creates rooms with specific naming convention: `"Chat: {name}"`

**4. Message Display (Element Web Standard):**

The actual message rendering uses Element Web's built-in components. Standard Element Web message display includes:
- **CSS classes** (Element Web standard): `mx_EventTile`, `mx_EventTile_info`, `mx_Message`, etc.
- **Styled-components:** Element Web uses SCSS/CSS, not styled-components
- Messages are rendered with user avatars, timestamps, and message body content
- Message styling is not customized in this HiClaw codebase; it uses Element Web's defaults

**5. Matrix Protocol Integration:**
- The Matrix channel plugin is at `/worksp/hiclaw/workspace/extensions/matrix/`
- Uses `matrix-js-sdk` (v41.6.0) for client-side Matrix operations
- Messages are sent/received through Matrix protocol (`/_matrix/client/v3/rooms/.../send`)

### Data Flow for Messages:

```
User types in Element Web (DOM input)
  → POST to Tuwunel /_matrix/client/v3/rooms/{roomId}/send
  → Tuwunel stores message in RocksDB
  → hiclaw-manager Matrix channel plugin receives sync event
  → OpenClaw gateway routes to AI agent
  → Agent generates response via LLM
  → Response sent back via Matrix
  → Element Web receives via sync and renders using built-in components
```

### File Paths Summary:

| Component | File Path | Type |
|-----------|-----------|------|
| Element Web startup | `/worksp/hiclaw/start-element-web.sh` | Bash script |
| Auto-login script | `/opt/element-web/auto-login.js` | Injected JS |
| Auth UI tweaks | `/opt/element-web/auth-ui-tweaks.js` | Injected JS |
| Control panel button | `/opt/element-web/control-panel-btn.js` | Injected JS |
| New chat button | `/opt/element-web/new-chat-btn.js` | Injected JS |
| Chat API backend | `/opt/element-web/hiclaw-chat-api.py` | Python HTTP server |
| Matrix plugin | `/worksp/hiclaw/workspace/extensions/matrix/` | OpenClaw plugin |
| Element Web config | `/opt/element-web/config.json` (generated at runtime) | JSON config |

The actual message rendering components are from the upstream Element Web application, not custom-built in this repository. Element Web uses React and its standard message components have CSS classes like `mx_EventTile` for styling message appearance.
